name: pandas
summary: Fast, flexible data analysis and manipulation for Python

story.problem: Working with tabular data in plain Python means nested lists, manual column tracking, slow loops, and reimplementing group-by, merge, and pivot from scratch every time. Spreadsheets don't scale; SQL requires a database.
story.mental_model: A DataFrame is a spreadsheet in memory — rows are observations, columns are variables. You select, filter, transform, and aggregate using column names instead of indices. Every operation returns a new DataFrame, so transformations chain cleanly.
story.when_to_use: Exploratory data analysis, cleaning CSV/Excel files, joining datasets, computing aggregations, and any time your data fits in RAM (up to a few GB).
story.when_not_to_use: Data larger than RAM — use Polars, Dask, or Spark instead. Real-time streaming — use a message queue. Pure numerical computation — NumPy is faster without the overhead.

alternatives.name: polars
alternatives.reason: 10-100x faster, lazy evaluation, same mental model, better for large files

alternatives.name: dask
alternatives.reason: Pandas API on out-of-core or distributed data

alternatives.name: numpy
alternatives.reason: Pure numerical arrays; no column names, but much faster for maths

nodes.label: pandas
nodes.summary: Top-level import. Alias as `pd` by convention.

nodes.label: DataFrame
nodes.summary: 2D labelled table. The core data structure — rows × columns.

nodes.label: Series
nodes.summary: 1D labelled array. A single column or row of a DataFrame.

nodes.label: Index
nodes.summary: Row labels. Default is 0,1,2… but can be dates, strings, or multi-level.

nodes.label: read_csv()
nodes.summary: Load a CSV file into a DataFrame. Most common entry point.
nodes.signature: read_csv(filepath, sep=',', header=0, index_col=None, dtype=None, ...) -> DataFrame

nodes.label: read_excel()
nodes.summary: Load an Excel workbook sheet into a DataFrame.

nodes.label: groupby()
nodes.summary: Split DataFrame into groups by column value, then aggregate.

nodes.label: merge()
nodes.summary: SQL-style join of two DataFrames on a key column.

nodes.label: apply()
nodes.summary: Apply a function row-by-row or column-by-column.

nodes.label: pivot_table()
nodes.summary: Create a spreadsheet-style pivot table with custom aggregation.
nodes.signature: pivot_table(data, values, index, columns, aggfunc='mean') -> DataFrame

steps.label: Inspect
steps.code:
print(df.head())      # first 5 rows
print(df.shape)       # (rows, cols)
print(df.dtypes)      # column types
print(df.describe())  # stats summary

steps.label: Inner join
steps.explanation: Like SQL INNER JOIN — only rows with a match in both tables.

steps.label: Left join
steps.explanation: Keep all orders, even those with no matching customer.

steps.label: Check result
steps.code:
print(result.shape)
print(result.isnull().sum())  # nulls from unmatched rows

steps.label: Find missing
steps.code:
df.isnull().sum()           # count NaN per column
df[df['age'].isnull()]      # rows where age is missing
